$comment: The app's SHAPE vocabulary — the companion to tokens.contract.json.
The token contract stopped the two surfaces' COLOURS from drifting apart. Nothing
stopped their SHAPES, and a design system drew them by hand, from reading this
source, with nothing checking the reading. Two of the first four were wrong within
a week: the inbox row was described as flat hairline-separated rows (the marketing
site's idiom — `0.5px` appears nowhere in this package) when the real row is a
bordered card in a spaced list, and the state pattern was generalised from the two
warning callouts in LLMSettings into a rule most state surfaces do not follow.
So the reading is written down and checked from both ends:
`evidence` — the FULL class attribute of the element that carries the shape. It
must be present, verbatim, in `source`. Checked HERE, on the commit that changes
the component.
It is the WHOLE attribute and not a substring on purpose. The first version of
this check tested substrings, and a review broke it in four ways in one sitting:
appending `border-2` to the inbox row left the substring intact (the row went to
2px while the design system kept drawing 1px, ✓), and moving the evidence into an
HTML comment while replacing the real markup with a flat `divide-y` list passed
too — the original lie, re-landed, with the guard applauding. A substring check is
orthogonal to whether the shape is still LIVE. The whole attribute is not, and
comments are stripped before matching.
`preview` — what the design system's product page must DRAW. Its declarations are
resolved last-wins across every rule with that selector, so appending an
overriding rule cannot slip a different shape past. Checked THERE, against this
file, fetched over https (this repo is public).
WHAT THIS DOES NOT CATCH, said plainly so nobody trusts it further than it goes: it
checks that the element still carries these classes, not that the element is still
rendered, reachable, or in the place the design system shows it. A shape can be
hidden by a parent, or moved, and this will not know. It catches restyling, which is
what actually happens.
It names no other repository and no private path: it is a list of class names
checked against this package's own components.

chat-user.what: The user's turn is a bubble. It is the only thing in the chat that is.
evidence: class="rounded-[var(--radius-md)] px-4 py-2.5 text-sm max-w-[80%] text-left cursor-pointer hover:opacity-80 transition-opacity {msg.failed ? 'bg-danger/10 border border-danger/30 text-danger' : msg.queued ? 'bg-bg-muted border border-border text-text-muted' : 'bg-accent/10 border border-accent/20'}"

chat-assistant.what: The assistant's turn has no container at all — but it carries the AI-disclosure badge, which is not optional (EU AI Act Art. 50(1)).
evidence: class="ai-badge float-left mr-2 mt-[3px] inline-flex items-center rounded-[var(--radius-sm)] border border-accent/40 bg-accent/10 px-1.5 py-0.5 text-[10px] font-mono uppercase tracking-widest text-accent-text"

chat-tool.what: A tool call is a left rule, never a card — but once it settles it earns a full-strength tick.
evidence: class="flex items-center gap-2 md:gap-1.5 text-[13px] md:text-[11px] text-text-subtle/70 border-l-2 {isError ? 'border-danger/40' : isRunning ? 'border-warning/40' : 'border-border'} pl-3 py-1 md:py-0.5"

chat-tool-tick.what: The tick is full strength — the rule is for glancing, the tick is for when you actually look.

inbox-list.what: A SPACED list — 2px apart (space-y-0.5). Not hairline-separated rows.

inbox-row.what: A bordered card — --radius-sm, 1px border, transparent at rest so nothing shifts when it lights up.
evidence: class="rounded-[var(--radius-sm)] border transition-colors {isActiveSelection ? 'border-accent bg-accent/5' : 'border-transparent hover:border-border hover:bg-bg-subtle/60'}"

inbox-row-selected.what: Selection is the loudest thing on the screen: the accent at full strength, over a 5% wash.

inbox-bar.what: The toolbar sits on a 40% wash of the subtle ground — enough to separate, not enough to announce itself.
evidence: class="border-b border-border bg-bg-subtle/40 px-4 py-2 flex items-center gap-2"

state.what: The DOMINANT state surface: a tint, a border of the SAME colour at low opacity, and the text in the semantic colour. (A minority of surfaces — five, all warning — use a full-strength border instead; see `stateExceptions`.)
evidence: class="rounded-[var(--radius-md)] bg-danger/10 border border-danger/20 px-4 py-3 text-sm text-danger mb-4"

checkbox.what: The one real primitive. 16px, and it keeps a visible focus ring — in the accent-TEXT role, because the accent fill is too dark to read as an outline on the dark ground.

$comment: The design system says the app's state surfaces are a tint + a same-colour border
at LOW opacity + the text in the semantic colour. That is the dominant pattern, not
a universal, and the last two attempts to write it as a universal were both wrong in
opposite directions. So the exceptions are counted, and the count is checked.
`fullStrengthSemanticBorders` — every `border-danger` / `border-warning` /
`border-success` at FULL strength in src/lib. Three of the danger ones are a badge
and two buttons (not state surfaces at all); the five warning ones are real state
surfaces and are the genuine exception. Success never does it.
If this number moves, the design system's prose about 'the app almost never does
this' has to move with it — which is the only reason it is here.
Facts the design system states in prose. Prose rots silently; these do not.
Scope is the LIBRARY — src/lib/components + src/lib/primitives, i.e. what pro
actually imports. The standalone app's own routes/ are not part of it (there is
a seventh <style> block in routes/login, and counting it here would make the
design system describe a surface no consumer of this package ever renders).
